mnemonics: P.E.N. Power: Power, Effect size, and N (sample size) are the three key ingredients. Increase any one to increase power.
B.E.A.N.S. for Factors: Beta (power), Effect size, Alpha, Noise (variance), and Sample size.
keyPoints: Statistical power (1 - β) is the probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis (i.e., detecting a real effect).
A larger sample size (n) increases power, reduces variability, and allows detection of smaller effect sizes.
Power is also increased by a larger true effect size and a higher significance level (α), though raising α increases the Type I error rate.
commonMistakes: Confusing statistical significance (p-value < α) with practical significance (the effect size is large enough to matter in the real world).
Calculating sample size AFTER an experiment is run ('post-hoc' power analysis), which is misleading. Sample size should be determined before data collection.
